title: Getting started with modelblueprint
text: predict() applies full pipeline — pre-processing, feature engineering, model prediction, post-processing — always returns numeric vector.

title: Getting started with modelblueprint
text: one_way() shows exposure-weighted mean target across bins feature, giving quick view marginal relationship. Pass predictions = TRUE overlay model’s -sample predictions lift chart.
code: one_way(mb, var = "wt") one_way(mb, var = "wt", predictions = TRUE)   # lift chart

title: Getting started with modelblueprint
text: pdp() reveals marginal effect feature model output, controlling features averaging predictions across training data.
code: pdp(mb, var = "wt", bins = 8L) #> ℹ Calculating pdp for `wt`

title: Getting started with modelblueprint
code: # Gains chart with Gini coefficient gain(mb) # Predicted vs observed calibration pred_vs_obs(mb) # Grouped residuals with loess trend residuals_grouped(mb, exposure_per_bin = 5)

title: Getting started with modelblueprint
text: filter(), mutate(), left_join() operate blueprint’s internal datasets return new modelblueprint — original never mutated.
code: mb_filtered <- mb |>   filter(cyl %in% c(4, 6)) |>   mutate(wt_kg = wt * 453.6)  nrow(mb_filtered@train) #> [1] 18

title: Getting started with modelblueprint
text: Use feat_eng_fun apply transformations prediction — function applied training data new data prediction time.
code: mb_fe <- modelblueprint(   model        = lm(mpg ~ wt + wt2, data = transform(mtcars, wt2 = wt^2)),   feat_eng_fun = function(df) transform(df, wt2 = wt^2),   train        = mtcars,   y_name       = "mpg" )  head(predict(mb_fe, mtcars)) #> [1] 22.91314 21.14211 25.19169 19.01764 17.76063 17.65463

title: Getting started with modelblueprint
text: savemb() serialises complete blueprint — model, data, pipeline functions — .tar.gz archive. loadmb() restores exactly.
code: savemb(mb, path = tempdir(), filename = "logistic_vs") #> modelblueprint saved: /tmp/RtmpLszwU8/logistic_vs.tar.gz mb2 <- loadmb(file.path(tempdir(), "logistic_vs.tar.gz"))  # Predictions are identical all.equal(predict(mb, mtcars), predict(mb2, mtcars)) #> [1] TRUE

This #> warning will become an error in a future version.  # All work with the same diagnostic functions one_way(mb_rf, var = "wt", predictions = TRUE) pdp(mb_xgb, var = "hp") #> ℹ Calculating pdp for `hp` gain(mb_glm)

title: Interactive model dashboard
text: dashboard depends three packages listed Suggests. Install already:
code: install.packages(c("shiny", "bslib", "plotly"))  # Optional --- adds loading spinners on slow plots install.packages("shinycssloaders")

title: Interactive model dashboard
text: Build modelblueprint least one data split call mb_dashboard(). app opens browser; R session stays blocked close window. model implements predict() works — lm, glm, rpart, randomForest, xgboost, H2O models supported box. built-example constructors ?mb_examples provide ready-made objects quick experimentation:
code: mb <- modelblueprint(   model              = glm(vs ~ wt + hp + am, data = mtcars, family = binomial),   train              = mtcars[1:24, ],   test               = mtcars[25:32, ],   y_name             = "vs",   x_original_inputs  = c("wt", "hp", "am"),   model_display_name = "logistic_vs" ) mb_dashboard(mb) mb_dashboard(mb_glm_binomial()) mb_dashboard(mb_rf_regression())

title: Interactive model dashboard
text: Summary tab: model card, dataset card, variable list, target/prediction density overlay. first tab shows model card dataset card side side, followed variable list target-vs-predicted density overlay. Model card — class, display name, target, exposure column, deploy notes stored object. Datasets card — row count, sum target, sum exposure split (train / test / holdout). Missing splits show —. Variables — original input features (@x_original_inputs) engineered features (@x_names) stored blueprint. Distribution plot — overlaid density histograms observed target model’s -sample predictions. well-calibrated model similar shapes. dataset selector bin slider card header update chart reactively.

what: Large models and the prediction cache
title: Interactive model dashboard
text: H2O, XGBoost, model single predict() call full dataset takes several seconds, dashboard unusably slow chart computed predictions independently. gain, predicted-vs-observed, residuals, distribution, one-way overlay charts trigger separate full-dataset scoring pass. mb_dashboard() avoids per-set prediction cache: first chart needs predictions given split computes stores result; every subsequent chart session reads cache. notification appears computation runs UI appear frozen. precomputed_preds argument available every individual diagnostic function, can take advantage cache working outside dashboard :
code: # The cache is transparent --- launch the dashboard as normal. # For a large H2O model: mb_dashboard(mb_h2o_glm_large(n = 50000L)) preds <- predict(mb, mtcars)  gain(mb,               set = "train", precomputed_preds = preds) pred_vs_obs(mb,        set = "train", precomputed_preds = preds) residuals_grouped(mb,  set = "train", precomputed_preds = preds) one_way(mb, var = "wt", predictions = TRUE, precomputed_preds = preds)

title: Interactive model dashboard
text: mb_dashboard() returns shiny.appobj. deploy , save blueprint disk saveMB() create self-contained app.R loads : my_app/ folder can published : Since package yet CRAN, pin dependencies renv deploying remote server installs correct version: H2O models require JVM target server. rocker/shiny RUN apt-get install -y default-jdk added Dockerfile reliable path; H2O shinyapps.io recommended.
code: saveMB(mb, path = "my_app/", filename = "model") # my_app/app.R library(modelblueprint) mb <- loadMB("model.tar.gz") mb_dashboard(mb) renv::init()       # in my_app/ renv::snapshot()   # captures modelblueprint and all dependencies

title: Model diagnostics
text: modelblueprint provides four diagnostic charts evaluating model performance. works plain data frame dispatches automatically modelblueprint object.

title: Model diagnostics
text: gains chart measures well model rank-orders risk. Predictions sorted highest lowest, chart shows proportion total observed losses captured move portfolio. perfect model captures losses top-risk segment. random model follows diagonal. Gini coefficient summarises rank-ordering ability single number -1 1 — higher better. use : gains chart primary performance metric insurance pricing. Gini 0.3 generally considered useful pricing; 0.5 strong.
code: mb <- mb_glm_binomial() mtcars$pred_model1 <- predict(mb, mtcars) + rnorm(nrow(mtcars)) mtcars$pred_model2 <- predict(mb, mtcars) + rnorm(nrow(mtcars))  # Gains chart — Gini shown in legend gain(mb)  # Compare two models on the same chart gain(   mtcars,   pred     = c("pred_model1", "pred_model2"),   obs      = "vs" )

title: Model diagnostics
text: calibration chart bins predictions plots average predicted rate average observed rate per bin. well-calibrated model points close diagonal — predicted observed equal average. chart three elements: Yellow bars — exposure per bin (right axis) Blue dots — observed rate per bin Dashed black line — predicted rate per bin dots track dashed line closely, model well-calibrated. Systematic divergence tails indicates model underestimates overestimates extreme risks. use : Use fitting check predicted rates match observed rates across full prediction range. Also useful comparing recalibrated vs uncalibrated model.
code: mb <- mb_glm_binomial()  # Calibration chart pred_vs_obs(mb, ret = "data")  # More bins for finer resolution pred_vs_obs(mb, bins = 20L)  # Equal-range bins (useful when predictions are skewed) pred_vs_obs(mb, type_agg = "equal_range")

title: Model diagnostics
text: residuals chart bins predictions exposure plots grouped residuals — difference observed predicted — loess trend line 95% confidence interval. well-specified model residuals scattered randomly around zero. systematic pattern indicates model misspecification. exposure_per_bin argument controls many data points go group — smaller values give groups noisier detailed view. use : Use diagnose model systematically - -predicts. upward trend right tail means model underestimates high-risk predictions; downward trend means overestimates.
code: mb <- mb_glm_binomial()  # Raw residuals (obs - pred) residuals_grouped(mb)  # Pearson residuals — scaled by sqrt(pred), useful for count/rate models residuals_grouped(mb, residual_type = "pearson")  # Control grouping granularity via exposure per bin residuals_grouped(mb, exposure_per_bin = 500)

title: Model diagnostics
text: SAMI chart compares two competing models binning ratio one model’s predictions another. bin ratio, shows observed mean alongside model predictions. ratio far 1, models disagree substantially. observed line reveals model closer reality region. plain data frame predictions already attached: recalib = TRUE, predictions scaled match observed mean computing ratios — isolates shape differences overall level differences. use : SAMI useful deciding two candidate models. shows just model performs better overall, one model better much. regions observed line tracks one model closely drive decision.
code: # Compare two blueprints mb1 <- mb_lm_regression() mb2 <- mb_glm_regression()  sami(list(mb1, mb2), bins = 10L) df        <- mtcars df$pred1  <- predict(mb1, mtcars) + rnorm(nrow(mtcars)) df$pred2  <- predict(mb2, mtcars) + rnorm(nrow(mtcars))  sami(df, obs = "mpg", pred = c("pred1", "pred2"),      exposure = "exposure", bins = 5, recalib = TRUE) sami(list(mb1, mb2), bins = 10L, recalib = TRUE)

title: One-way analysis plots
text: one-way plot shows exposure-weighted mean one target variables across bins single feature. standard diagnostic understanding univariate relationship feature target insurance credit pricing. chart dual-axis layout: Yellow bars (right axis) — exposure per bin, showing data density Lines (left axis) — weighted mean target variable per bin

title: One-way analysis plots
text: can also call one_way() directly data frame:
code: library(modelblueprint)  mb <- mb_glm_binomial()  # Default: 35 equal-exposure bins, target from y_name slot one_way(mb, var = "wt") one_way(mtcars, var = "wt", obs = "vs", exposure = "exposure")

title: One-way analysis plots
text: Pass predictions = TRUE add model’s -sample predictions second line. creates lift chart — gap observed predicted lines reveals model - -fits. prediction column named model_display_name appears legend alongside observed target.
code: one_way(mb, var = "wt", predictions = TRUE)

title: One-way analysis plots
text: Pass character vector obs overlay several lines chart. Useful comparing two competing models raw target smoothed version.
code: # Compare two model predictions directly one_way(   mtcars,   var      = "wt",   obs      = c("vs", "am"),   # two lines   exposure = "exposure" )

title: One-way analysis plots
text: bins argument controls many bins x-axis divided .
code: # Coarser view — 10 bins one_way(mb, var = "wt", bins = 10L)  # Finer view — 50 bins one_way(mb, var = "wt", bins = 50L)

title: One-way analysis plots
text: type_agg controls bins formed: "equal_exposure" (default) — quantile-based bins, roughly equal total exposure. Preferred skewed distributions. "equal_range" — evenly-spaced bins across feature range. Better visualising shape relationship tails.
code: one_way(mb, var = "wt", type_agg = "equal_range", bins = 5)

title: One-way analysis plots
text: split argument breaks bin groups, producing one line per group. Useful comparing behaviour across segments policy type, region, claim flag.
code: one_way(mb, var = "mpg", split = "am", bins = 5)   # split by transmission type

title: One-way analysis plots
text: calling modelblueprint, use set choose internal dataset plot. Comparing one-way train vs test quick check overfitting.
code: one_way(mb, var = "wt", set = "train") one_way(mb, var = "wt", set = "test")

title: One-way analysis plots
text: Pass ret = "data" get aggregated data.table instead plot. Useful custom visualisations analysis. returned data.table columns bin label, split group, exposure, one column per obs variable.
code: d <- one_way(mb, var = "wt", ret = "data") head(d)

title: One-way analysis plots
text: One-way plots handle categorical low-cardinality integer variables automatically — binning applied level appears bar. Levels 2,000 unique values trigger warning return NULL.
code: mtcars$cyl_f <- as.character(mtcars$cyl) one_way(mtcars, var = "cyl_f", obs = "vs", exposure = "exposure")

what: What is a partial dependence plot?
title: Partial dependence plots
text: partial dependence plot (PDP) reveals marginal effect single feature model output, averaging influence features. Unlike one-way plot — shows raw observed predicted means per bin — PDP fixes feature series values averages predictions across full training distribution. makes PDPs especially useful : Understanding non-linear feature effects black-box models Comparing shape relationship across competing models Diagnosing interactions unexpected behaviour chart dual-axis layout one-way plots: Yellow bars (right axis) — exposure per bin Three lines (left axis) — observed mean, -sample predicted mean, PDP (marginal effect)

title: Partial dependence plots
text: plain data frame:
code: library(modelblueprint)  mb <- mb_glm_binomial()  # PDP for wt — everything pulled from blueprint slots pdp(mb, var = "wt") model <- glm(vs ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial) pdp(mtcars, var = "wt", obs = "vs", model = model)

title: Partial dependence plots
text: PDP chart shows three lines per feature: Observed — exposure-weighted mean actual target per bin. one-way plot. Predicted (-sample) — exposure-weighted mean predict(model, data) per bin. Tracks observed line model well-calibrated. PDP — true marginal effect. bin midpoint, feature fixed value across rows training data predictions averaged. isolates feature’s contribution correlations features. PDP line diverges predicted line, signals feature’s -sample predictions driven partly correlations rather feature .

title: Partial dependence plots
text: PDP computation requires predicting across full dataset bin. large datasets, sample_size caps number rows used: default 10,000. models, 1,000 rows gives stable PDP line fraction computation cost.
code: # Coarser view pdp(mb, var = "wt", bins = 5L)  # Finer view — slower for large models pdp(mb, var = "wt", bins = 20L) # Use at most 1,000 rows for PDP computation pdp(mb, var = "wt", sample_size = 1000L)

title: Partial dependence plots
code: pdp(mb, var = "wt", type_agg = "equal_range")

text: models per-call overhead (H2O HTTP round-trips, remote endpoints, XGBoost DMatrix construction) typically 5-20x faster calling predict() per bin loop. Memory cost: sample_size * n_bins rows. 10,000 rows x 15 bins 150,000 rows — well within reason typical dataset.

title: Permutation-based SHAP value computation — compute_shap
text: feature j vars observation , runs nsim random permutations vars feature ordering. permutation s: vars features appearing j ("coalition") keep actual values; vars features j swapped background row without_j, with_j keeps j actual swaps features j. Non-vars columns always retain actual observation values model can find every column trained . marginal contribution pred(with_j) - pred(without_j). Averaging permutations gives unbiased Shapley value estimate.

text: Bins feature variable, computes exposure-weighted means one observed variables per bin, returns dual-axis plotly chart (bars = exposure, lines = weighted means). Optionally splits second variable.

title: Create a one-way analysis plot — one_way
code: one_way(data, ...)  # Default S3 method one_way(   data,   var,   obs = "target",   exposure = "exposure",   split = NA_character_,   bins = 35L,   time_unit = NA_character_,   type_agg = c("equal_exposure", "equal_range"),   ret = c("plot", "data"),   ... )

title: Create a one-way analysis plot — one_way
text: data data.frame data.table. ... Arguments passed methods. var Column plot x-axis. Bare name string. obs One columns summarise y-axis (right axis). Bare name, c(, b), character vector. Default "target". exposure Column exposure weights. column exist data, every row given weight 1. Bare name string. Default "exposure". split Optional column split lines . Bare name, string, NA / NULL split. Default NA. bins [integer(1)] Number equal-exposure bins numeric variables bins unique values. Default 35. time_unit [character(1)] Date / POSIXct variables, width bin. Passed directly base::cut.POSIXt(), string accepts works: "month", "week", "2 weeks", "12 hours", "quarter", "year", etc. Ignored non-date columns. Default NA (date binning — dates shown individual values). type_agg [character(1)] Binning strategy numeric variables: "equal_exposure" (default) "equal_range". ret [character(1)] "plot" (default) returns plotly object; "data" returns aggregated data.table.

title: Create a one-way analysis plot — one_way
text: plotly object, data.table ret = "data", NULL warning plot produced.

title: Create a one-way analysis plot — one_way
text: Column name arguments (var, obs, exposure, split) accept bare (unquoted) names strings, one_way(df, wt, mpg) one_way(df, "wt", "mpg") equivalent.

title: Create a one-way analysis plot — one_way
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # bare names one_way(mtcars, wt, mpg, bins = 10) one_way(mtcars, cyl, c(mpg, hp), split = am)  # strings (equivalent) one_way(mtcars, var = "wt", obs = "mpg", bins = 10) one_way(mtcars, var = "cyl", obs = c("mpg", "hp"), split = "am") } # }

what: Partial dependence plot for any predict()-compatible model — pdp
title: Partial dependence plot for any predict()-compatible model — pdp
text: bin var, function fixes feature bin midpoint (numeric) bin label (categorical), runs predict() across sample full dataset, averages predictions. result shows marginal effect var model output, stripped correlations features.

title: Partial dependence plot for any predict()-compatible model — pdp
code: pdp(data, ...)  # Default S3 method pdp(   data,   var,   obs,   model,   exposure = "exposure",   bins = 10L,   sample_size = 10000L,   type_agg = c("equal_exposure", "equal_range"),   model_name = "model",   ret = c("plot", "data"),   pre_process_fun = function(df) df,   feat_eng_fun = function(df) df,   post_process_fun = function(preds, df_raw) preds,   seed = 2024L,   ... )

title: Partial dependence plot for any predict()-compatible model — pdp
text: data data.frame data.table. ... Arguments passed methods. var [character(1)] Feature column vary x-axis. obs [character(1)] Observed target column name. model fitted model object. Standard R models (lm, glm, xgb, ranger, tidymodels workflows, etc.) H2O models supported automatically - extra arguments needed. exposure [character(1)] Exposure weight column. absent, every row given weight 1. Default "exposure". bins [integer(1)] Number bins numeric var. Default 10. sample_size [integer(1)] Rows sample PDP computation. Reducing speeds prediction cost accuracy. Default 10,000. full dataset always used one-way actuals. type_agg [character(1)] Binning strategy: "equal_exposure" (default) "equal_range". model_name [character(1)] Label shown plot legend. Default "model". ret [character(1)] "plot" (default) returns plotly object; "data" returns aggregated data.table. pre_process_fun function(df) -> df applied data feature engineering. Default identity function. feat_eng_fun function(df) -> df (matrix) applied pre-processing produce model input. Default identity function. post_process_fun function(preds, df_raw) -> numeric applied raw model predictions. Default identity function. seed [integer(1)] Seed PDP row sample, applied via withr::with_seed() global RNG stream left undisturbed. Default 2024L.

title: Partial dependence plot for any predict()-compatible model — pdp
text: plotly object, data.table ret = "data", NULL warning variable plotted.

title: Partial dependence plot for any predict()-compatible model — pdp
text: Alongside PDP line chart also shows: Observed mean per bin (actual target, exposure-weighted) Model average prediction per bin (-sample, PDP) Global average observed predicted reference lines Yellow exposure bars (left axis) - identical style one_way()

title: Partial dependence plot for any predict()-compatible model — pdp
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ m <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp + cyl, data = mtcars)  # Basic usage pdp(mtcars, var = "wt", obs = "mpg", model = m)  # GLM - predict() is dispatched automatically g <- glm(vs ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial) pdp(mtcars, var = "wt", obs = "vs", model = g)  # Return aggregated data instead of a plot pdp(mtcars, var = "wt", obs = "mpg", model = m, ret = "data") } # }

what: Render SHAP feature importance chart — plot_shap_importance
title: Render SHAP feature importance chart — plot_shap_importance
text: Horizontal bar chart mean SHAP (signed) per feature, sorted magnitude (mean |SHAP|) influential feature top. Purple bars indicate feature whose average effect increases prediction; blue bars indicate feature decreases . zero reference line colour legend subtitle make direction immediately readable.

title: Render SHAP feature importance chart — plot_shap_importance

title: Render SHAP feature importance chart — plot_shap_importance
text: Styling matches pdp() one_way(): transparent background, margins, hovermode = "y unified".

what: Place sorted-order bin labels back into the original row positions — remap_sorted_bins

text: bin_equal_exposure() bin_equal_range() operate sorted, non-NA values vector. Given labels sorted order plus precomputed ordering non-NA positions, writes label back original row, leaving NA entries NA_character_. Callers decide label missing values (e.g. trailing "NA" category).

what: Grouped Residuals vs Predicted Plot — residuals_grouped
title: Grouped Residuals vs Predicted Plot — residuals_grouped

code: sami(data, ...)  # Default S3 method sami(   data,   obs,   pred,   bins = 50L,   exposure = "exposure",   type_agg = c("equal_exposure", "equal_range"),   recalib = FALSE,   ret = c("plot", "data"),   ... )  # S3 method for class 'list' sami(   data,   set = c("train", "test", "holdout"),   bins = 20L,   type_agg = c("equal_exposure", "equal_range"),   recalib = FALSE,   pred_names = NA_character_,   ret = c("plot", "data"),   ... )

text: data list modelblueprint objects. Must length 2 . blueprints must share y_name, expo_name, training data structure. ... Default "train". pred_names [character] Optional vector names prediction columns. Length must match length(data). NA, names derived model_display_name.

what: SHAP Feature Importance and Dependence Plots — shap
title: SHAP Feature Importance and Dependence Plots — shap
text: Computes approximate SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values using built-permutation algorithm returns either feature importance chart (signed mean SHAP per feature  - purple = increases prediction, blue = decreases prediction, sorted magnitude) dependence plot (mean SHAP per bin alongside exposure bars). plots use dual-axis Plotly style one_way() pdp().

title: SHAP Feature Importance and Dependence Plots — shap
code: shap(data, ...)  # Default S3 method shap(   data,   model,   vars,   exposure = "exposure",   type = c("importance", "dependence"),   nsim = 50L,   sample_size = 500L,   bins = 10L,   type_agg = c("equal_exposure", "equal_range"),   ret = c("plot", "data"),   model_name = "model",   pre_process_fun = function(df) df,   feat_eng_fun = function(df) df,   post_process_fun = function(preds, df_raw) preds,   seed = 2024L,   ... )

title: SHAP Feature Importance and Dependence Plots — shap
text: data data.frame data.table. Must contain columns named vars , optionally, exposure. ... Unused. model fitted model object. vars [character] Feature column names SHAP values computed. exposure [character(1)] Exposure weight column. column absent, every row given weight 1. Default "exposure". Used dependence plot (exposure bars weighted mean SHAP line). type [character(1)] Plot type: "importance" (default) returns signed horizontal bar chart mean SHAP per feature (purple = positive effect, blue = negative effect, sorted |SHAP| magnitude); "dependence" returns dual-axis chart (exposure bars + mean SHAP per bin) variable vars. nsim [integer(1)] Number random permutations per observation. Higher values give stable SHAP estimates cost compute time. Default 50L. sample_size [integer(1)] Rows sampled data SHAP computation. Default 500L. Seeded 2024 reproducibility. bins [integer(1)] Number bins dependence plot x-axis. Default 10L. type_agg [character(1)] Binning strategy dependence plot: "equal_exposure" (default) "equal_range". ret [character(1)] "plot" (default) "data". "data" returns data.table one SHAP column per feature vars. model_name [character(1)] Label shown plot titles. Default "model". pre_process_fun function(df) -> df applied feat_eng_fun. Default identity function. feat_eng_fun function(df) -> df (matrix) produces model input. Default identity function. post_process_fun function(preds, df_raw) -> numeric applied raw model predictions. Default identity function. seed [integer(1)] Seed row sample internal SHAP permutations, applied via withr::with_seed() global RNG stream left undisturbed results reproducible. Default 2024L.

title: SHAP Feature Importance and Dependence Plots — shap
text: plotly object, data.table (ret = "data"), named list plotly objects type = "dependence" vars one element.
